executed_trades.trade_frequency_estimate: HEAVILY REDUCED — 69% of past trades would have been blocked

3_hard_floor_impact.note_on_discrepancy: Executed-trade pass rate (30.9%) is higher than shadow-rec pass rate (13.9%) because bridge filtering (age gate, edge gate, open-position cap) already preferentially suppresses lower-confidence signals. Higher-confidence signals tend to have higher edge and fresher data, so they survive bridge filtering at higher rates.

5_recommendation_specific_view.why_executed_pass_rate_is_higher_than_re: Bridge filtering (rec_age gate, edge threshold, open-position cap) acts as a pre-filter that is positively correlated with confidence. High-confidence signals tend to have stronger model edges and fresher game-state data, so they survive bridge filtering at a higher rate. Many low-confidence shadow recs are rejected by the bridge before ever becoming a trade — but enough still get through that 69% of executed trades would fail a 0.60 floor.
5_recommendation_specific_view.model_confidence_bimodal_structure: Confidence values show a strongly bimodal distribution. The bulk of signals cluster at 0.33–0.40 (low-conviction signals, early-game, score-neutral states). A second cluster sits at 0.62–0.65 (high-conviction signals: team leads, late-game leverage states, strong pregame edge). Very few signals land in the 0.50–0.60 zone. This means a confidence floor anywhere between 0.50 and 0.62 produces similar trade frequency — the gap between clusters is sparse.

6_conclusion.is_0_60_realistic: YES — but with a significant trade frequency reduction that must be accepted intentionally

expected_impact.on_executed_trade_frequency: ~69% reduction from historical baseline — roughly 1 in 3 of previously executed trades would be permitted
expected_impact.on_raw_recommendation_flow: ~86% reduction — only 1 in 7 model signals would survive the floor
expected_impact.qualitative: HEAVILY REDUCED — this is not a minor filter. It is a substantial quality gate that changes the system from a moderate-frequency trader to a low-frequency, high-conviction-only trader.

6_conclusion.structural_insight: The model produces bimodal confidence — mostly 0.33–0.40 or 0.62–0.65. Very little signal lands in 0.50–0.60. So the choice between 0.55, 0.58, and 0.60 floors has almost the same practical outcome: all three cut the same population of trades. A 0.55 floor adds roughly +3–4 ppt pass rate vs 0.60 (brings the total from ~31% to ~35% of trades). The practical difference is small.

safer_alternatives_if_frequency_is_too_l.note: Current setting. Meaningful quality gate. Accepts the high-conviction cluster only.
Adds the 0.55–0.599 bin. Only ~+4 ppt more trades. Not a material frequency change.
Adds 0.50–0.599. Still blocks the dominant low cluster. ~+8 ppt more trades.

6_conclusion.current_live_trade_alert: The 2 currently open live trades (id 223: conf=0.3353, id 224: conf=0.3996) were entered under the old system with no confidence floor. Both fall well below 0.60. They will run to their existing TP/SL/exit conditions regardless. The new gate is entry-only — it does not affect positions already open.
6_conclusion.recommendation: Keep 0.60. The frequency reduction is real and large but the bimodal structure means lowering the floor to 0.55 or 0.58 recovers almost nothing. If trade frequency is too low, the right lever is broadening the market scope (more game lines, other sports) rather than lowering the confidence threshold.
